quiz_question: What fraction of Earth's total surface does the Pacific Ocean cover?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Pacific Ocean covers approximately 32% of the planet's total surface area — exceeding the combined land area of all continents (148,000,000 km²). It is the largest division of the World Ocean.

quiz_question: What is the name of Earth's deepest known point, located in the Pacific?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Challenger Deep in the Mariana Trench (northwestern Pacific) reaches 10,928 meters (35,853 feet). The Pacific also contains the second-deepest point: the Horizon Deep in the Tonga Trench at 10,823 meters.

quiz_question: What is the average (mean) depth of the Pacific Ocean?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Pacific Ocean's mean depth is 4,000 meters (13,000 feet). Combined with its immense area of 165,250,000 km², it holds by far the largest volume of water of any ocean.

quiz_question: Which two nations jointly ruled Vanuatu before its 1980 independence?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, in 1906 France and the United Kingdom agreed to jointly manage the archipelago as the New Hebrides through an Anglo-French condominium — a rare shared colonial administration. Vanuatu gained independence in 1980.

quiz_question: Which creole language is one of Vanuatu's three official tongues?
explanation: According to Wikidata, Vanuatu's three official languages are English, French, and Bislama. Bislama is an English-based creole that serves as a common tongue across Vanuatu's many island communities, each with their own distinct local language.

quiz_question: In what year did the first Europeans arrive in Vanuatu on a Spanish expedition?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, a Spanish expedition led by Portuguese navigator Fernandes de Queirós arrived on the largest island, Espíritu Santo, in 1606. He claimed the archipelago for Spain, naming it La Austrialia del Espíritu Santo — believing he had found a great southern continent.

quiz_question: What did early European explorers call Samoa because of its people's seafaring abilities?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, because of the Samoans' seafaring skills, pre-20th-century European explorers referred to the entire island group, including American Samoa, as the 'Navigator Islands'. The Lapita people first settled the islands around 3,500 years ago.

quiz_question: Which country colonized Samoa from 1899 until it was occupied at WWI's start?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Samoa became a German colony in 1899 after the Tripartite Convention. German administration ended in August 1914 when New Zealand troops bloodlessly occupied the colony at the start of World War I.

quiz_question: What year did Samoa gain independence — the first Pacific Island nation to do so in the 20th century?
explanation: According to Wikipedia and Wikidata (inception: 1962-01-01), Western Samoa gained independence on 1 January 1962, after being administered as a UN Trust Territory by New Zealand. It officially reverted to the name 'Samoa' on 4 July 1997.

quiz_question: In what year was Kyoto first established as Japan's imperial capital?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Kyoto was chosen in 794 CE as Japan's imperial capital by Emperor Kanmu. The emperors ruled from Kyoto for eleven centuries until 1869, when the capital moved to Tokyo after the Meiji Restoration — a reign of over 1,075 years.

quiz_question: Which globally famous video game company is based in Kyoto, Japan?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the internationally renowned video game company Nintendo is based in Kyoto. The ancient cultural capital is also home to Kyoto University, the second-oldest university in Japan.

quiz_question: Why does Kyoto retain more prewar cultural heritage than most Japanese cities?
correct_answer: Because it was spared in WWII
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Kyoto was spared from large-scale destruction during World War II, which is why its prewar cultural heritage — Buddhist temples, Shinto shrines, palaces, gardens — has mostly survived unlike many other Japanese cities.

quiz_question: What makes Tonga unique among Pacific island nations in colonial history?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, from 1900 to 1970 Tonga had British protected-state status, but the UK only managed Tonga's foreign affairs under a Treaty of Friendship. Tonga never relinquished its sovereignty to any foreign power — unique among Pacific island nations.

quiz_question: What type of empire was the ancient Tuʻi Tonga — built on control of what?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Tuʻi Tonga grew into a regional thalassocracy — an empire based on sea power — controlling territories from the Solomon Islands and New Caledonia in the west to parts of modern-day French Polynesia in the east.

quiz_question: Of Tonga's 171 islands, how many are actually inhabited?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Tonga's 171 islands have a total surface area of about 750 km², but are scattered over 700,000 km² of ocean — the ocean area is nearly 1,000 times larger than the land. 70% of Tongans live on the main island Tongatapu.

quiz_question: What is the official capital of Palau, distinct from its largest city Koror?
explanation: According to Wikipedia and Wikidata, Palau's capital is Ngerulmud, located on the largest island of Babeldaob in Melekeok State. Koror, on a separate island, is the most populous city and former capital.

quiz_question: The brutal WWII Battle of Peleliu took place on an island in which modern Pacific nation?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Battle of Peleliu was fought between American and Japanese troops as part of the Mariana and Palau Islands campaign in WWII. Peleliu is one of Palau's administrative states. Palau gained full sovereignty in 1994 under a Compact of Free Association with the US.

quiz_question: Approximately how many islands make up Palau, the world's 16th smallest country?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Palau has a total area of 466 km² spread across approximately 340 islands, making it the sixteenth smallest country in the world. Its capital Ngerulmud sits on Babeldaob, Palau's largest island.

quiz_question: What was Sydney's original purpose when founded by the British in 1788?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, in 1788 the First Fleet of convicts, led by Arthur Phillip, founded Sydney as a British penal colony — the first European settlement in Australia. The traditional custodians of the land are the clans of the Darug, Dharawal and Eora.

quiz_question: How long have Aboriginal Australians inhabited the Greater Sydney region?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, there is evidence that Aboriginal Australians inhabited the Greater Sydney region at least 30,000 years ago. The traditional custodians are the clans of the Darug, Dharawal and Eora, whose rock engravings and cultural sites are still common today.

quiz_question: What major global event did Sydney host in the year 2000?
explanation: According to Wikipedia and Wikidata, Sydney hosted the 2000 Summer Olympics. The city's Kingsford Smith Airport is one of the world's oldest continually operating airports, and Sydney ranks among the top 15 most-visited cities worldwide.

quiz_question: What is Tuvalu's total land area, making it one of Earth's smallest countries?
explanation: According to Wikipedia and Wikidata, Tuvalu's total land area is 25.14 km² spread across three reef islands and six atolls. Its 2022 census population was 10,643, making it the 194th most populous country — exceeding only Niue and Vatican City.

quiz_question: What does the name 'Tuvalu' mean in the Tuvaluan language?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Tuvalu means 'eight standing together' in the local language, referencing the eight traditionally inhabited atolls and islands. The nation actually has nine islands total, but one was historically uninhabited.

quiz_question: What were Tuvalu's islands called under British colonial rule before independence?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Tuvalu was previously known as the Ellice Islands and managed with the Gilbert Islands as one British colony. A 1974 referendum led to separation, and Tuvalu became fully independent on 1 October 1978.

quiz_question: What makes Kiribati's eastern islands unique in global timekeeping?
correct_answer: UTC+14, first to see each day
distractors: Second to greet each new day
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the International Date Line was redrawn around Kiribati, swinging far east, to put its easternmost Line Islands in the same calendar day as the Gilbert Islands — placing them in UTC+14, the world's most advanced time zone.

quiz_question: How does Kiribati uniquely span all four hemispheres simultaneously?
distractors: Has land in Europe and Asia
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Kiribati's islands straddle the equator (crossing northern and southern hemispheres) and the 180th meridian (crossing eastern and western hemispheres). No other country crosses both of these global dividing lines.

quiz_question: On which Fijian island do most displaced Kiribati Banabans now live in exile?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, prior to independence Kiribati exported phosphate from Banaba Island, but the mine is now virtually exhausted. The descendants of the Banabans mostly live in exile on Rabi in Fiji but are represented in the Kiribati Parliament.

quiz_question: What single feature covers more of Earth than all land areas combined?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Pacific Ocean covers approximately 32% of the planet's total surface area — exceeding all land area on Earth combined (148,000,000 km²). At 165,250,000 km², this single ocean is larger than every continent, island, and desert put together.

quiz_question: Why are ancient shipwrecks exceptionally preserved in the Black Sea's depths?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Black Sea has a permanent anoxic (oxygen-free) deep layer that does not drain or mix with surface water. This absence of oxygen prevents the biological decay that would normally destroy organic material, leaving ancient wooden ships perfectly preserved on the seafloor.

quiz_question: How many countries does the Black Sea's drainage basin touch?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, while only Bulgaria, Georgia, Romania, Russia, Turkey, and Ukraine have direct coastlines on the Black Sea, rivers draining into it collect runoff from 24 countries across Europe. The Danube alone passes through or borders 10 nations.

quiz_question: Through the Bosporus, which direction does the deeper, saltier water flow?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Bosporus carries two simultaneous water flows: less dense freshwater from the Black Sea exits on top, while denser, more saline water from the Aegean Sea flows in underneath. This two-layer current is a direct result of the Black Sea's positive water balance from river inflows.

quiz_question: Which territory holds the record as the most densely populated on Earth?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Macau (32.9 km²) houses approximately 720,000 people, making it the most densely populated territory in the world. Two-thirds of its total area was reclaimed from the sea — meaning the natural land footprint is even smaller.

quiz_question: How does Macau's gambling industry compare to Las Vegas in size?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Macau's casino gambling industry generates revenue approximately seven times that of Las Vegas, Nevada. After casino licences were liberalized in the early 2000s, Macau overtook Las Vegas to become the world's top gambling destination.

quiz_question: In what year did Portugal hand Macau back to China?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Ming dynasty first leased Macau to Portugal as a trading post in 1557 — the first permanent European trading base in China. Portugal held it for 442 years until the 1999 handover, after which Macau became a special administrative region under 'one country, two systems.'

quiz_question: How many years did Marco Polo live inside Kublai Khan's empire?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Marco Polo (c. 1254–1324) traveled through Asia along the Silk Road between 1271 and 1295, spending 17 years living in the emperor's lands. His book provided the first Western record of porcelain, gunpowder, paper money, and various Asian plants and animals.

quiz_question: Where did Marco Polo dictate his famous book of travels?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, after returning from Asia, Marco Polo was captured during a war between Venice and Genoa. While imprisoned, he dictated his tales to cellmate Rustichello da Pisa. The resulting book, 'The Travels of Marco Polo' (c. 1300), inspired explorers including Christopher Columbus.

quiz_question: Which of these did Marco Polo's writings introduce to Western knowledge first?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Marco Polo's account included the first Western written records of porcelain, gunpowder, paper money, and various Asian plants and exotic animals. His narrative inspired Christopher Columbus and many other explorers, and he influenced European cartography through the Catalan Atlas and Fra Mauro map.

quiz_question: How many Himalayan peaks exceed 7,200 meters above sea level?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Himalayas contain more than 100 peaks exceeding 7,200 meters (23,600 feet), including the highest, Mount Everest at 8,848.86 meters. This concentration of extreme altitude is unmatched by any other range on Earth.

quiz_question: Why are certain Himalayan peaks like Gangkhar Puensum permanently off-limits to climbers?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, summits of several Himalayan peaks — including Kangchenjunga (from the Indian side), Gangkhar Puensum, Machapuchare, Nanda Devi, and Kailash — are off-limits due to their sacred status in Hinduism and Buddhism. Gangkhar Puensum in Bhutan is the world's highest unclimbed peak.

quiz_question: How long is the Himalayan mountain range in kilometres?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Himalayan range runs 2,400 km (1,500 mi) from west-northwest to east-southeast, abutting or crossing Nepal, India, China, Bhutan, and Pakistan. Its western anchor is Nanga Parbat; its eastern anchor is Namcha Barwa.

quiz_question: Which country has islands in UTC+14, the world's most advanced time zone?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Kiribati's easternmost islands — the southern Line Islands — sit in UTC+14, the world's most advanced time zone. The International Date Line was redirected far eastward specifically to keep all of Kiribati in the same calendar day, placing these islands 26 hours ahead of Baker Island (UTC-12).

quiz_question: Which is the only country whose territory crosses both the equator and the International Date Line?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Kiribati's islands spread from Banaba in the west to Kiritimati in the east, straddling both the equator and the 180th meridian where the International Date Line runs. No other nation occupies this geographic intersection.

quiz_question: How does Kiribati's ocean territory compare to its actual land area?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Kiribati has a total land area of only 811 km² but its islands are dispersed across 3,441,810 km² of ocean — roughly 4,245 times more ocean than land. This makes Kiribati one of the most dispersed nations on Earth by ocean-to-land ratio.

quiz_question: What fraction of Earth's water surface does the Pacific Ocean cover?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Pacific Ocean covers approximately 46% of Earth's water surface and about 32% of the planet's total surface area — making it larger than all of Earth's land area (148 million km²) combined.

quiz_question: Which city is home to Nintendo's headquarters — an ancient capital now housing a gaming giant?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the internationally renowned video game company Nintendo is based in Kyoto. This means the same city that served as Japan's imperial capital for over a thousand years is now also home to one of the world's most famous entertainment companies.

quiz_question: How many Himalayan peaks exceed 7,200 metres above sea level?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Himalayas have more than 100 peaks exceeding 7,200 metres (23,600 feet) above sea level. The range runs 2,400 km long and separates the Indian subcontinent from the Tibetan Plateau.

quiz_question: How many people live in the combined drainage basin of rivers fed by the Himalayas?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, some of the world's major rivers — the Indus, the Ganges, and the Tsangpo–Brahmaputra — rise near the Himalayas, and their combined drainage basin is home to some 600 million people, with 53 million living in the Himalayas themselves.

quiz_question: What distinction does the Port of Shanghai hold globally?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Port of Shanghai is the world's busiest container port. As of 2025, the city has a population of 29,558,908 in the urban area, making it the most populous urban area in China.

quiz_question: How does Macau's gambling industry compare in size to Las Vegas?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Macau's gambling industry is around seven times larger than that of Las Vegas, Nevada. It is also the most densely populated territory on Earth, with most of its land area reclaimed from the sea.

quiz_question: What was the unusual founding population of Sydney in 1788?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, in 1788 the First Fleet of convicts, led by Arthur Phillip, founded Sydney as a British penal colony — the first European settlement in Australia. Evidence suggests Aboriginal Australians inhabited the region at least 30,000 years before this.

quiz_question: How many years was Marco Polo away from Venice on his famous journey to Asia?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Marco Polo traveled the Silk Road between 1271 and 1295, serving as Kublai Khan's emissary for 17 years within China and the empire. He returned to Venice after 24 years away, only to be captured in a war with Genoa — and dictated his famous travel accounts from prison.

quiz_question: What is the total land area of Tuvalu?
explanation: According to Wikipedia and Wikidata, Tuvalu's total land area is 25.14 square kilometres (9.71 sq mi), comprising three reef islands and six atolls. The 2022 census recorded a population of just 10,643 — smaller than most small towns.

quiz_question: Which city's subway is the longest metro network by route length?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Shanghai Metro first opened in 1993 and has since grown to become the largest metro network in the world by route length. It grew rapidly in the 21st century during the city's economic boom.

quiz_question: Shanghai's urban area population in 2025 was approximately how many million?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Shanghai's urban area reached 29,558,908 people as of 2025. Originally a fishing village, it grew to global prominence after the First Opium War opened it as a treaty port in the 19th century.

quiz_question: Which sovereign nation has the highest water-to-land ratio, with 98% of its territory being ocean?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, 98.13% of the Marshall Islands' territory is water, the largest proportion of water to land of any sovereign state. The country consists of 29 coral atolls and five main islands, plus 1,220 other very small islands.

quiz_question: How many islands were joined together to form the city of Mumbai?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Mumbai was originally seven islands that were connected through land reclamation projects beginning in the 18th century. Today the city is the financial capital of India with over 12.5 million people.

quiz_question: Which Indian city is both the financial capital and the most populous city proper?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Mumbai is simultaneously the financial capital and most populous city proper of India. It hosts the Bombay Stock Exchange, the National Stock Exchange, and is home to Bollywood, the world's largest film industry by output.

quiz_question: How many historical cities have been built on the site of modern Delhi?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Delhi has been the capital of various empires for over a millennium. Archaeological and historical evidence shows at least eight historical cities built at or near the current location, including famed sites like Qutb Minar from the Delhi Sultanate era.

quiz_question: What ranking does Delhi hold among the world's largest urban agglomerations?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Delhi is India's most populous metropolitan area and the second-largest urban agglomeration in the world. The National Capital Territory straddles the Yamuna River and is home to New Delhi, India's capital.

quiz_question: Which combined landmass is the largest contiguous continental area on Earth?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Eurasia is the combined landmass of Europe and Asia, recognized as the largest contiguous landmass on Earth. It is home to approximately 70% of the world's human population.

quiz_question: What approximate percentage of Earth's total land surface does Siberia cover?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Siberia covers approximately 13.1 million km² of northern Russia, representing about 9% of Earth's total land area. Despite this enormous size, fewer than 40 million people live there.

quiz_question: What mountain range forms the western boundary of Siberia?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Siberia extends from the Ural Mountains in the west to the Pacific Ocean in the east. This makes Siberia coincide with the entirety of North Asia, a vast subcontinent-scale region.

quiz_question: Which Indian Ocean island near Madagascar is legally part of France and the EU?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Réunion has been a French overseas department since 1946. Located about 679 km east of Madagascar, it is one of the outermost regions of the European Union — making it technically part of Europe despite being in the southern Indian Ocean.

quiz_question: When French colonists arrived in the 17th century, what was the population of Réunion?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Réunion had no human inhabitants before French colonial subjects arrived in the 17th century. Its plantation economy then imported enslaved people from East Africa, followed by indentured laborers from Asia, creating today's diverse mixed population.

quiz_question: How many of Central Asia's five major countries are landlocked?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, all five Central Asian countries are landlocked, giving the region the world's highest concentration of such nations. Uzbekistan is doubly landlocked — surrounded entirely by other landlocked countries, one of only two such nations on Earth.

quiz_question: The Cook Islands' ocean exclusive zone is how many times larger than its land area?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Cook Islands' land area is just 236.7 km², but their Exclusive Economic Zone covers 1,960,027 km² of ocean — about 8,280 times larger. This gives the tiny nation vast maritime resources and fishing rights.

quiz_question: Which canal connects the Red Sea to the Mediterranean Sea?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Red Sea is a sea inlet of the Indian Ocean lying between Africa and Asia. The Suez Canal at its northern end connects it to the Mediterranean, creating a direct shipping route from Europe to Asia that avoids sailing around Africa.

quiz_question: Why is Southeast Asia considered a global biodiversity hotspot?
correct_answer: Because of its dense species concentration
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Southeast Asia is bordered by China, South Asia, and Australia, and its diverse habitats — from tropical rainforests to coral reefs — support a disproportionately high number of species relative to its land area, making it one of Earth's key biodiversity hotspots.

quiz_question: Which Indian city was the second largest in the British Empire, surpassed only by London?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Calcutta was the second-largest city in the British Empire — after London — and served as the de facto capital of British India until 1911, when the capital was moved to New Delhi. It was also the centre of the Indian independence movement.

quiz_question: How long ago were the Solomon Islands first settled by humans?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Solomon Islands have been settled since at least some time between 30,000 and 28,800 BC. Later waves of migrants — notably the Lapita people — mixed with earlier settlers to produce the modern indigenous population. This predates most famous ancient civilizations by tens of thousands of years.

quiz_question: On which Solomon Island is the capital Honiara located?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Honiara is located on Guadalcanal, the largest island in the Solomon Islands. During World War II, the Solomon Islands campaign (1942–1945) saw fierce fighting between the United States, British Imperial forces, and Japan — including the pivotal Battle of Guadalcanal.

quiz_question: How far does the Federated States of Micronesia span from east to west?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the FSM consists of about 607 islands that span approximately 2,700 km (some sources cite ~2,900 km) across the western Pacific. Despite this enormous stretch, total land area is only about 702 km² — smaller than many individual cities.

quiz_question: Which FSM state contains the world's largest concentration of sunken WWII warships?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Chuuk (formerly Truk) is one of the four states of the FSM. During Operation Hailstone in February 1944, the US destroyed a massive Imperial Japanese Navy fleet in Chuuk Lagoon — now considered the world's premier wreck diving destination, with over 60 sunken ships.

quiz_question: How many independent countries are entirely enclosed within Italy's borders?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Italy is home to two sovereign enclaves: Vatican City (within Rome) and San Marino (in the Apennines). Italy is one of the world's few countries to contain two completely independent nations within its territory.

quiz_question: Approximately how many lakes does Finland contain?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Finland has approximately 188,000 lakes of at least 500 square meters. This gives it one of the highest lake densities of any country. The nickname 'Land of a Thousand Lakes' undersells the actual count by about 187 times.

quiz_question: How long is Norway's coastline including fjords and islands?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Norway has a coastline of approximately 100,000 km when fjords and islands are measured. This extreme length is due to the deep glacially carved fjords that penetrate far inland, making it one of the longest coastlines in the world.

quiz_question: What happened to Poland's borders after World War II?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, after WWII, Poland lost its eastern Kresy region to the Soviet Union and gained former German territories (Silesia, Pomerania, part of East Prussia) in the west. This effectively moved the entire country westward — one of history's most dramatic geopolitical border shifts.

quiz_question: Which Balkan country declared itself the world's first officially atheist state under communism?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, under Enver Hoxha's communist regime, Albania declared itself the world's first officially atheist state in 1967 and banned all religious practice. Churches and mosques were converted into sports halls or warehouses. This lasted until the fall of communism in 1991–1992.

quiz_question: What tectonic process is slowly widening the Red Sea over millions of years?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Red Sea was formed by the Arabian plate rifting away from the African plate. This seafloor spreading continues today and will eventually transform the Red Sea into a full ocean — the same process that once created the Atlantic Ocean.

quiz_question: How many hours apart are American Samoa and independent Samoa in time zones?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, American Samoa (UTC-11) and independent Samoa (UTC+13) are separated by 25 hours. Samoa switched sides of the International Date Line in 2011, creating this extreme timezone split with its American neighbor — even though the two territories are visible from each other.

quiz_question: Approximately how many people live in Niue, one of the world's least populous nations?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Niue has a tiny resident population of around 1,500 people — making it one of the least populated nations on Earth. Yet it controls a vast exclusive economic zone because ocean territory is assigned based on political sovereignty, not population size.

quiz_question: What percentage of the world's known nickel reserves does New Caledonia hold?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, New Caledonia contains approximately 25% of the world's nickel reserves. This makes the French special collectivity — a Pacific island group of just 270,000 people — one of the most economically significant mining territories on Earth.

quiz_question: Which larger country are the Cook Islands in 'free association' with?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Cook Islands is a self-governing nation in free association with New Zealand. Cook Islanders are New Zealand citizens and can freely move to New Zealand, but the Cook Islands has full powers of self-government in both domestic and foreign affairs.

quiz_question: Approximately how many people live in East Asia?
explanation: According to Wikidata, East Asia has a population of approximately 1,616,547,247 people. The region includes China, Japan, South Korea, North Korea, Taiwan, Mongolia, Hong Kong, and Macau — with China alone accounting for nearly 90% of the total.

quiz_question: How many constituent nations make up the United Kingdom?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the UK consists of four countries: England, Scotland, Wales, and Northern Ireland. Scotland, Wales, and Northern Ireland have their own devolved legislatures, while England is governed directly by the UK Parliament at Westminster — a quirk that creates the 'West Lothian Question.'

quiz_question: How many countries make up Southeast Asia?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Southeast Asia consists of 11 countries: Brunei, Cambodia, East Timor, Indonesia, Laos, Malaysia, Myanmar, Philippines, Singapore, Thailand, and Vietnam. The region has a population of over 680 million and extraordinary linguistic and cultural diversity.

quiz_question: How many years was Germany divided into East and West before reunification?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Germany was divided following WWII in 1945 and reunified on October 3, 1990. The Berlin Wall fell on November 9, 1989, and reunification followed less than a year later. Germany is now the most populous country in the European Union.

quiz_question: Which Indian city was the second-largest in the entire British Empire?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Calcutta was the de facto capital of British India until 1911 and the second-largest city in the British Empire after London. It was the center of bureaucracy, politics, law, education, science, and the arts in India.

quiz_question: Which Indian city contains the country's only Chinatown?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Kolkata is unique in India for hosting the country's only Chinatown. The community was established by Hakka Chinese immigrants during British rule and preserves Chinese temples, schools, and culinary traditions to this day.

quiz_question: What is the population of Kolkata's wider metropolitan region?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Kolkata's metropolitan area has over 15 million inhabitants, making it the third most populous metropolitan region in India after Mumbai and Delhi. The city proper population is about 4.5 million.

quiz_question: Which landmass contains both the world's highest and lowest exposed land points?
explanation: According to Wikidata, Eurasia's highest point is Mount Everest and its lowest is the Dead Sea at approximately −430 m. This gives Eurasia a vertical range of more than 9,000 metres — no other single landmass spans such an extreme elevation difference.

quiz_question: Which modern Japanese metropolis briefly served as the imperial capital in the 7th–8th centuries?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Osaka developed into an important regional port by the Kofun period (300–538 CE) and briefly served as Japan's imperial capital in the 7th and 8th centuries — long before Edo (modern Tokyo) rose to prominence.

quiz_question: Which Japanese city earned the Edo-period nickname 'Kitchen of the Realm'?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Osaka's pseudonym 天下の台所 ('Kitchen of the realm') dates to the Edo period, when the city was Japan's central hub for rice, fish, and goods distribution. This culinary legacy defines Osaka's identity to the present day.

quiz_question: Which Pacific French territory is named after Scotland?
explanation: According to Wikidata, New Caledonia was named after Scotland (Caledonia being the Latin name for Scotland). The island was thought to resemble the Scottish Highlands. It remains a French special collectivity in the southwest Pacific, 1,210 km east of Australia.

quiz_question: What fraction of the Niuean population lives in New Zealand rather than on Niue?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, between 90% and 95% of all Niuean people live in New Zealand, with fewer than 2,000 remaining on the island. Niue is a self-governing state in free association with New Zealand, and Niueans hold New Zealand citizenship.

quiz_question: Which European country was first to grant universal suffrage, in 1906?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Finland became the first country in Europe to grant universal suffrage in 1906, giving all adult citizens — men and women — not only the vote but also the right to stand for public office. This right to run for office was a world first.

quiz_question: Which country has the world's largest sovereign wealth fund?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Norway's Government Pension Fund Global — funded by North Sea petroleum revenues — is valued at over US$2.1 trillion, making it the world's largest sovereign wealth fund. Norway is the world's largest producer of oil and gas outside the Middle East on a per-capita basis.

quiz_question: Which US territory has the highest rate of military enlistment per capita?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, American Samoa is noted for having the highest rate of military enlistment of any US state or territory. As of 2021, the local US Army recruiting station in Pago Pago ranked first nationally in recruitment.

quiz_question: Kolkata (Calcutta) served as the capital of British India for most of the colonial period. In what year did the British move the capital to Delhi?
explanation: The British moved India's colonial capital to Delhi in 1911 to strengthen their hold over the interior and reduce the influence of Bengal's independence movement. Kolkata had been the seat of British power since 1690.

quiz_question: Kolkata is home to Tiretti Bazaar, which holds the distinction of being what in India?
correct_answer: The only traditional Chinatown in India
distractors: The only walled city remaining in South Asia
India's only open-air museum dedicated to Chinese art
The only neighborhood in India where Cantonese is an official street language
India's first designated UNESCO heritage market
The oldest Buddhist trading post in South Asia
The only Indian neighborhood founded by Hakka Chinese migrants
explanation: Tiretti Bazaar in Kolkata is India's only traditional Chinatown, a remnant of 18th-century Chinese merchant settlements that brought traders, silk merchants, and artisans to the city. It remains a unique cultural enclave with Chinese restaurants and shops.

quiz_question: Kolkata is one of India's most populous metropolitan areas. Approximately how many people live in the Kolkata urban agglomeration?
explanation: Kolkata's metropolitan area is home to around 14 million people, making it one of India's largest cities. Despite economic decline since British rule ended, the region remains a cultural and intellectual hub of India.

quiz_question: Eurasia is the world's largest landmass. What percentage of Earth's total land area does it cover?
explanation: Eurasia covers about 36% of Earth's total land area, spanning from Portugal to Japan and from the Arctic to Southeast Asia. This single landmass contains over 60% of the world's population.

quiz_question: Eurasia contains both the world's highest point and the world's lowest point on land. What are they?
correct_answer: Mount Everest (highest) and the Dead Sea shore (lowest)
distractors: Mount Everest (highest) and the Caspian Sea depression (lowest)
K2 (highest) and the Dead Sea shore (lowest)
Mount Elbrus (highest) and the Dead Sea shore (lowest)
Mount Everest (highest) and the Aral Sea basin (lowest)
Mount Everest (highest) and the Mariana Trench (lowest)
explanation: Mount Everest (the world's highest peak at 29,032 feet) and the Dead Sea shore (the world's lowest point on land at 1,410 feet below sea level) are both located in Eurasia, creating an extreme elevation range within a single continent.

quiz_question: Osaka was once an ancient capital of Japan. What was the city historically called during its time as imperial capital?
explanation: Osaka was historically called Naniwa when it served as an imperial capital in ancient Japan. The name dates back to the 7th century and reflects the city's ancient importance as a trading port.

quiz_question: Osaka has a famous nickname in Japan that reflects its reputation as a city of food culture. What is it?
correct_answer: Tenka no Daidokoro — 'Kitchen of the Nation' (or 'Nation's Kitchen')
distractors: The City of a Thousand Flavors
The City of Merchants and Spices
The Rice Bowl of Western Japan
The Foodie City of the East
explanation: Osaka earned the nickname 'Tenka no Daidokoro' (Kitchen of the Nation) for its reputation as Japan's culinary capital, famous for street food, okonomiyaki (savory pancakes), and innovative regional cuisine that reflects its merchant trading heritage.

quiz_question: New Caledonia in the Pacific Ocean was named after which European country, whose Latin name is 'Caledonia'?
explanation: British explorer James Cook named New Caledonia after Scotland, whose Latin name is 'Caledonia.' The archipelago's resemblance to the Scottish Highlands inspired the name when Cook encountered it in 1774.

quiz_question: Niue, a tiny Pacific island nation, has a remarkable population situation. Approximately what percentage of Niueans live in New Zealand rather than Niue itself?
explanation: Approximately 90% of Niueans live abroad, primarily in New Zealand, making Niue one of the world's most diaspora-heavy nations. Only about 1,600 people remain on the island itself, a consequence of limited economic opportunities and cyclone devastation.

quiz_question: Finland was the first country in the world to grant women full political rights, including the right to stand for election. In what year?
explanation: Finland became a global pioneer in 1906 by granting women the right to vote AND stand for election, making it the first country worldwide to offer women full political participation. This came 14 years before women could even vote in the United States.

quiz_question: Finland is known as the 'Land of a Thousand Lakes'. Approximately how many lakes does it actually have?
explanation: Finland has approximately 180,000 lakes (not just 1,000), earning its 'Land of a Thousand Lakes' nickname. These glacially-formed lakes cover about 10% of Finland's land area and are central to Finnish culture and identity.

quiz_question: Norway manages the world's largest sovereign wealth fund, built from oil revenues. Approximately how much is it worth?
explanation: Norway's Government Pension Fund Global is worth over 1 trillion USD, built from North Sea oil revenues. It's the world's largest sovereign wealth fund, investing in companies globally and generating returns to support Norway's future welfare state.

quiz_question: The Kingdom of Norway traces its origins to approximately 872 CE. Who is traditionally credited with unifying Norway into a single kingdom?
explanation: Harald Fairhair (Harald Hårfagre) is credited with unifying Norway around 872 CE, defeating rival chieftains in the Battle of Hafrsfjord. This victory established the Norwegian kingdom that would dominate Scandinavia for centuries.

quiz_question: Italy is unique among European countries in that it contains two fully independent nations within its borders. What are they?
explanation: Vatican City and San Marino are both fully independent nations entirely surrounded by Italian territory. Vatican City is the spiritual heart of Catholicism, while San Marino is the world's oldest surviving republic, founded in 301 CE.

quiz_question: American Samoa has the highest military enlistment rate of any U.S. territory or state. Why is this notable given residents' legal status?
correct_answer: American Samoans are U.S. nationals (not citizens) but still enlist at the highest per-capita rate in the U.S.
distractors: American Samoans are the only U.S. territory residents who receive a military service bonus
American Samoa has mandatory military service unlike any U.S. state or territory
American Samoans have a special treaty requiring military service in exchange for territorial status
American Samoans are the only U.S. nationals who can vote in federal elections while serving
American Samoa has the most military bases per capita of any U.S. territory
American Samoans are classified as foreign nationals who voluntarily serve in the U.S. military
American Samoa has a military tradition dating to a formal defense alliance with the U.S. from 1900
American Samoans have full U.S. citizenship granted automatically upon military service
explanation: American Samoans have the highest military enlistment rate in the U.S. despite being U.S. nationals (not automatic citizens), reflecting deep military tradition and limited economic opportunities on the islands. Military service is often a pathway to citizenship.

quiz_question: In what year did Prussia unify Germany into the German Empire?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the German Empire was founded on 18 January 1871 when the south German states joined the North German Confederation. Prussia, containing about two-thirds of the empire's population and territory, dominated the new state under Kaiser Wilhelm I and Chancellor Otto von Bismarck.

quiz_question: Germany holds what rank among Europe's economies by nominal GDP?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Germany is the largest economy in Europe by nominal GDP. It also ranks as the world's third-largest exporter and third-largest importer, making it a dominant global trade force.

quiz_question: How many UNESCO World Heritage Sites does Germany have in total?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Germany ranks third globally for UNESCO World Heritage Sites with 55, of which 52 are cultural. This places Germany behind only Italy and China on the global list.

quiz_question: At its peak, the British Empire controlled roughly what fraction of Earth's landmass?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, at its height in the 1920s the British Empire encompassed around a quarter of the world's landmass and population, making it the largest empire in history. The UK became the first industrialised country and was the world's foremost power for most of the 19th and early 20th centuries.

quiz_question: Which country was the world's very first to industrialise?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the UK became the first industrialised country and was the world's foremost economic power for most of the 19th and early 20th centuries. Its industrial capacity supported a global empire and made London the world's principal financial centre.

quiz_question: Since what year has Switzerland not fought an international war?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Switzerland has maintained a policy of armed neutrality since the 16th century and has not fought an international war since 1815. It is also the birthplace of the Red Cross and hosts the headquarters of most major international institutions including the WHO, WTO, and WEF.

quiz_question: How many official languages does Switzerland recognize?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Switzerland encompasses four principal linguistic and cultural regions — German, French, Italian, and Romansh — reflecting a long-standing tradition of multilingualism. Despite this diversity, Swiss national identity remains cohesive; they call themselves a 'Willensnation' or 'nation of will'.

quiz_question: Which country is credited as the birthplace of democracy and the Olympic Games?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Greece is considered the cradle of Western civilisation and the birthplace of democracy, Western philosophy, Western literature, historiography, political science, theatre, and the Olympic Games.

quiz_question: Which country has the longest coastline on the Mediterranean basin?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Greece has the longest coastline on the Mediterranean basin, shaped by thousands of islands and nine traditional geographic regions. Athens is the capital and largest city, followed by Thessaloniki and Patras.

quiz_question: Which European country earned the nickname 'the Battlefield of Europe'?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Belgium's central location kept it contested and controlled by various European powers for centuries — Habsburg Spain (1556–1714), Austrian Habsburgs (1714–1794), Revolutionary France (1794–1815) — earning the nickname 'the Battlefield of Europe', reinforced by both World Wars.

quiz_question: Which city is considered the de facto capital of the European Union?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Brussels serves as the de facto capital of the EU, hosting the European Commission, the Council of the EU, the European Council, and one of two seats of the European Parliament (the other being Strasbourg). NATO is also headquartered in Brussels.

quiz_question: What percentage of Swedes live in urban areas despite the country's vast northern wilderness?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Sweden is the largest Nordic country by both area and population at 450,295 km², yet 88% of its 10.6 million people live in urban areas — mostly in the central and southern half. Urban areas cover just 1.5% of its total land.

quiz_question: Which country adopted Europe's first modern constitution in 1791?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth adopted Europe's first modern constitution in 1791 and was one of the great powers of Europe with an elective monarchy and a uniquely liberal political system. This came just months after the US Constitution took effect.

quiz_question: What is the world's smallest sovereign state by area?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Vatican City covers only 0.44 km² and is entirely enclosed within Rome, Italy. It serves as the administrative centre of the Catholic Church and is governed by the Pope.

quiz_question: How is the head of state of Vatican City chosen?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Vatican City is governed by the Holy See and the Pope holds absolute executive, legislative, and judicial authority. Unlike hereditary monarchies, the Pope is elected by the College of Cardinals in a conclave — making France (whose president is a co-prince of Andorra) the only republic that is also a monarchy.

quiz_question: Which European Union capital is the smallest by area and population?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Malta's capital Valletta is the smallest EU capital city by both area and population, in a country of about 574,250 people spread over just 316 km². Despite its tiny size Valletta was designated a European Capital of Culture and has been a fortress city since the 16th century.

quiz_question: Which country is the second-largest in Europe by area after Russia?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Ukraine is the second-largest country in Europe after Russia, which borders it to the east and northeast. Many people assume France or Germany holds this position, making Ukraine's scale a genuinely surprising geographic fact.

quiz_question: Which European country is jointly ruled by a French president and a Spanish bishop?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Andorra is a co-principality in the eastern Pyrenees, headed by two co-princes: the Bishop of Urgell in Catalonia, Spain, and the President of France. This arrangement was formalised by a charter in 1278 and has lasted over 700 years, making France the only republic that is technically also a monarchy.

quiz_question: Which Soviet republic was the first to declare independence from the USSR?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, on 11 March 1990, Lithuania became the first Soviet republic to break away when it proclaimed the restoration of its independence — a full year before the formal dissolution of the Soviet Union in 1991. Lithuanian armed resistance to the Soviet occupation had lasted into the early 1950s.

quiz_question: What language family does Hungarian belong to, unlike all its neighbours' tongues?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Hungarian is the official language of Hungary and belongs to the Uralic language family, outside the Indo-European group that encompasses its neighbours (German, Slovak, Romanian, Croatian, Serbian, Austrian, Ukrainian). Its closest relatives are Finnish and Estonian.

quiz_question: Ireland shares its only land border with which part of the United Kingdom?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Ireland consists of 26 of the 32 counties of the island of Ireland and its only land border is with Northern Ireland, which is part of the United Kingdom. This border became globally significant after Brexit as the only land frontier between the UK and the EU.

quiz_question: Which tiny country hosts the Court of Justice of the European Union?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Luxembourg City is one of four EU institutional seats and hosts the Court of Justice of the European Union — the highest judicial authority in the EU. Despite being one of Europe's smallest countries, Luxembourg punches above its weight as a legal and financial hub.

quiz_question: Which city is the most populous in Europe?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Moscow has a population estimated at over 13 million within city limits and over 21.5 million in its metropolitan area, making it the most populous city in Europe and the largest city entirely within Europe. It stands on the Moskva River in Central Russia.

quiz_question: Why was Paris originally given the nickname 'City of Light'?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Paris is nicknamed the 'City of Light' partly because of its role in the Age of Enlightenment, when it was a world centre of finance, diplomacy, culture, and intellectual thought. The electric lighting association came later, reinforcing an older intellectual nickname.

quiz_question: Which small Baltic country is globally renowned for its advanced digital government?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Estonia ranks highly in digital infrastructure, press freedom, and happiness. The country has pioneered e-government, e-residency, and digital voting, becoming a global model for digital public services despite its small size of about 1.3 million people.

quiz_question: Which sovereign state is the most densely populated on Earth?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Monaco's population of 38,423 is packed into only 2.08 km², making it the most densely populated country on Earth. Fewer than 10,000 of those residents are actually Monégasque nationals.

quiz_question: Which country holds the record for the world's shortest national coastline?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Monaco's coastline is only 3.83 km (2.38 mi) — the shortest of any non-landlocked nation. Vatican City and San Marino have no coastline at all, so Monaco uniquely holds this record among coastal states.

quiz_question: Which ruling family has governed Monaco since 1297?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the House of Grimaldi has ruled Monaco since 1297 — over 700 years — with only brief interruptions. Monaco's sovereignty was formally recognized by the Franco-Monégasque Treaty of 1861, and it became a UN voting member in 1993.

quiz_question: Liechtenstein is one of only two countries in the world with what unusual geographic property?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Liechtenstein is 'doubly landlocked' — it is surrounded by Austria and Switzerland, both of which are also landlocked. The only other doubly landlocked country is Uzbekistan. Liechtenstein covers just over 160 km² with a population of 41,389.

quiz_question: Which tiny Alpine principality is one of the few countries with no national debt?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Liechtenstein is 'one of the few countries with no debt.' It has a strong financial sector centred in its capital Vaduz, and was once known as a billionaire tax haven before making efforts to shed that reputation after a 2008 tax affair.

quiz_question: What geographic record does Liechtenstein hold as the world's smallest country?
distractors: Has no airport of its own
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Liechtenstein 'is the world's smallest country to border two countries' — those being Austria and Switzerland. It is Europe's fourth-smallest country at just over 160 km² and was formed in 1719.

quiz_question: Which small country claims to be the world's oldest surviving republic?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, San Marino 'claims to have been founded in AD 301,' making it arguably the world's oldest surviving republic. It is a landlocked country completely surrounded by Italy, sitting on Monte Titano in the Apennines, with a population of 34,042 as of 2025.

quiz_question: San Marino is entirely surrounded by which single country?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, San Marino is 'completely surrounded by Italy,' making it one of three countries entirely enclosed by a single nation — alongside Vatican City (also inside Italy) and Lesotho (inside South Africa). Its land area is just over 61 km².

quiz_question: Luxembourg holds what unique distinction among the world's monarchies?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Luxembourg is 'the world's only remaining sovereign grand duchy,' making it constitutionally unique among monarchies. It is a representative democracy headed by a constitutional monarch — Grand Duke Guillaume V as of 2025.

quiz_question: Approximately what fraction of Luxembourg's population are foreign nationals?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, 'foreign nationals account for almost half the population' of Luxembourg. In 2025 it had a population of 690,947 — making it one of Europe's least-populated countries but with its highest population growth rate.

quiz_question: Which Nordic country is the largest in both area and population?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Sweden at 450,295 km² is both the largest Nordic country and the fifth-largest in all of Europe. Despite its size, it has a low population density of just 25.5 people per km², with 88% of Swedes living in urban areas.

quiz_question: In what year did Sweden last fight a war — over 200 years ago?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, 'the last war in which Sweden was directly involved was in 1814,' when Sweden by military force brought Norway into a personal union that lasted until 1905. Since then Sweden has maintained over 200 years of continuous peace.

quiz_question: Which autonomous Arctic territory is part of the Kingdom of Denmark?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Denmark is 'the metropole and most populous constituent of the Kingdom of Denmark,' a constitutionally unitary state that also includes Greenland and the Faroe Islands as autonomous territories in the north Atlantic Ocean.

quiz_question: Which medieval state, centred on today's Ukraine, was the largest realm in Europe in the 10th–11th centuries?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Kievan Rus' emerged in the 9th century as a key centre of East Slavic culture and became the largest and most powerful realm in Europe in the 10th and 11th centuries before disintegrating and being destroyed by the Mongols in the 13th century.

quiz_question: Which two autonomous territories make up the Kingdom of Denmark alongside mainland Denmark?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Kingdom of Denmark is a constitutionally unitary state comprising metropolitan Denmark plus the Faroe Islands (home rule since 1948) and Greenland (home rule since 1979, further autonomy in 2009).

quiz_question: Of Denmark's roughly 1,400 islands, how many are actually inhabited?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Kingdom of Denmark (including the Faroe Islands and Greenland) counts roughly 1,400 islands over 100 sq m; 443 have been named and only 78 are inhabited. Copenhagen sits on the largest island, Zealand.

quiz_question: How many bridges connect the 126 islands that make up Venice?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Venice consists of 126 islands separated by expanses of open water and canals, and connected by 472 bridges. The city has been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1987.

quiz_question: Which medieval city is considered the world's first international financial centre?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Venice's city-state is considered to have been the first real international financial centre, emerging in the 9th century and reaching its greatest prominence in the 14th century — centuries before Amsterdam or London assumed that role.

quiz_question: Who ended the nearly thousand-year independence of the Republic of Venice in 1797?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Republic of Venice lasted from 810 to 1797 — nearly a millennium. Napoleon Bonaparte ended its independence in 1797. Venice later became part of the Kingdom of Italy in 1866.

quiz_question: How many peaks in the Alps rise above 4,000 metres?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Alps stretch about 1,200 km across eight Alpine countries (Monaco, France, Switzerland, Italy, Liechtenstein, Germany, Austria, Slovenia) and contain 82 peaks exceeding 4,000 m. Mont Blanc at 4,809 m is the highest.

quiz_question: How old was the Otzi mummy discovered on an Alpine glacier in 1991?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Otzi the Iceman was discovered in 1991 on a glacier at the Austrian-Italian border in the Alps. Analysis determined the body to be approximately 5,000 years old, making it one of the oldest and best-preserved natural human mummies ever found.

quiz_question: How many countries do the Alps stretch across?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Alpine arch stretches across eight countries: Monaco, France, Switzerland, Italy, Liechtenstein, Germany, Austria, and Slovenia. The range extends from Nice on the western Mediterranean to Trieste on the Adriatic and Vienna at the beginning of the Pannonian Basin.

quiz_question: How many times has Paris hosted the Summer Olympics?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Paris hosted the 1900 and 1924 Summer Olympics, as noted in its significant events, and also hosted the 2024 Games. This makes Paris one of the very few cities to host the Summer Olympics three times.

quiz_question: Why is Paris nicknamed the 'City of Light'?
correct_answer: Because of its role in the Enlightenment
distractors: First city with gas street lamps
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Paris is nicknamed the 'City of Light' (Ville Lumière) partly because of its role in the Age of Enlightenment, when it became a major centre of philosophy, arts, and intellectual thought across the 17th and 18th centuries.

quiz_question: Which country is jointly ruled by a bishop and a foreign president?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Andorra has been co-ruled since a 1278 charter — making the sitting French president automatically one of its heads of state. No other country in the world shares this arrangement.

quiz_question: What is the highest capital city in Europe by elevation?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Andorra la Vella sits at 1,023 metres (3,356 feet), outranking all other European capitals including Madrid (667 m) and Bern (542 m).

quiz_question: How many tourists visit Andorra each year despite its tiny population?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Andorra's ~89,000 residents are visited by roughly 8 million tourists annually — nearly 90 visitors for every single resident, one of the highest ratios in the world.

quiz_question: Which British territory sits at the southern tip of the Iberian Peninsula?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Gibraltar is a British Overseas Territory covering only 6.7 km², strategically placed at the entrance to the Mediterranean Sea, where it has been under British control since 1704.

quiz_question: Who led the first circumnavigation of Earth but died before it finished?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Magellan's expedition (1519–1522) was the first to circumnavigate the globe. Magellan was killed at the Battle of Mactan in the Philippines in 1521; Juan Sebastián Elcano completed the voyage in his place.

quiz_question: Which country is the largest located entirely within Europe?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Ukraine covers approximately 603,550 km², making it the largest country whose entire territory lies within the European continent. Russia, while larger, spans both Europe and Asia.

quiz_question: Which small Baltic nation allows anyone in the world to become a digital resident and run a business there remotely?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Estonia is a Baltic state in Northern Europe that became globally notable for pioneering e-governance, online voting, and digital residency programs after restoring independence in 1991.

quiz_question: Which Russian city was the imperial capital for over 200 years before Moscow?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Saint Petersburg was founded in 1703 by Peter the Great on the Baltic coast and served as Russia's imperial capital until 1918, when the Bolsheviks moved the capital back to Moscow.

quiz_question: Which tiny Mediterranean island nation was awarded the George Cross for its WWII resistance?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Malta's strategic location in the central Mediterranean made it vital to Allied operations. King George VI awarded the island the George Cross in 1942 for its civilian and military resistance under siege.

quiz_question: Which European country is often cited as having the oldest unchanged national borders?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Portugal's borders have remained essentially unchanged since it was recognized as an independent kingdom in 1143 and its current borders were finalized in 1297 — making it one of the oldest continuously bordered states in the world.

quiz_question: Which Eastern European country is bordered only by Romania and Ukraine?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Moldova is one of Europe's smallest countries, sandwiched between Romania to the west and Ukraine on all other sides, with the Prut River forming its western border.

quiz_question: Which Spanish city hosted the 1992 Summer Olympics and is the capital of Catalonia?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Barcelona is Catalonia's capital and Spain's second-largest city. It hosted the 1992 Summer Olympics, which transformed the city's waterfront and boosted its global profile.

quiz_question: Which two seas border Albania's coast?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Albania is located on both the Adriatic and Ionian Seas within the Mediterranean Sea. This dual-coast position gives Albania access to two distinct bodies of water despite being a relatively small Balkan country.

quiz_question: Which Albanian leader held off the Ottoman Empire for over 20 years?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Skanderbeg's military campaigns in the 15th century repelled Ottoman advances for over two decades, making him Albania's national hero. He is regarded as one of the great military commanders of medieval Europe.

quiz_question: How many states make up the Austrian federation?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Austria is a federation of nine states. Vienna is unique in functioning as both the national capital and its own federal state — the most populous of the nine.

quiz_question: In what year was Austria elevated to an Archduchy?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Austria emerged as a Duchy in 1156 and was made an Archduchy in 1453. This elevation reflected its growing status as the heartland of the Habsburg monarchy, which had dominated it since the late 13th century.

quiz_question: In what year did the Byzantine Empire formally recognize Bulgaria's First Empire?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Bulgars established the First Bulgarian Empire, recognized by treaty in 681 AD by the Byzantine Empire. This makes Bulgaria one of the oldest officially recognized states in medieval Europe.

quiz_question: Which medieval empire is credited with developing the Cyrillic script?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the First Bulgarian Empire dominated most of the Balkans and significantly influenced Slavic cultures by developing the Cyrillic script. This alphabet is now used by hundreds of millions of people across Russia, Ukraine, Serbia, and beyond.

quiz_question: What percentage of its historical territory did Hungary lose in the 1920 Treaty of Trianon?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Treaty of Trianon in 1920 established Hungary's current borders, resulting in the loss of 71% of its historical territory, most of its economy, 58% of its population, and 32% of its ethnic Hungarians. It remains one of the most dramatic post-war territorial losses in European history.

quiz_question: Into how many parts was Hungary divided after the Battle of Mohács in 1526?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Hungary was divided into three parts after the Battle of Mohács in 1526: Royal Hungary under the Habsburgs, Ottoman Hungary, and the semi-independent Principality of Transylvania. The Ottomans did not recognize their loss of Hungarian territory until the Treaty of Karlowitz in 1699.

quiz_question: What fraction of its population did Belarus lose during World War II?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, military operations devastated Belarus in World War II — the country lost about a quarter of its population and half of its economic resources, making it one of the most severely affected nations in the war.

quiz_question: Which Soviet republic joined the UN as a founder in 1945, even though it was not fully independent?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, in 1945 the Byelorussian SSR became a founding member of the United Nations, even though it was a Soviet republic rather than a fully sovereign state. The Soviets argued for separate representation due to Belarus's enormous WWII losses.

quiz_question: In what year was Croatia first internationally recognized as an independent state?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Croatia was first internationally recognized as independent on 7 June 879 during the reign of Duke Branimir. Tomislav later became the first king by 925, elevating Croatia to kingdom status.

quiz_question: Which explorer led the first circumnavigation of Earth but died before it finished?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Ferdinand Magellan led the first expedition to circumnavigate Earth but was killed in the Philippines in 1521 during the Battle of Mactan. His navigator Juan Sebastián Elcano completed the voyage with only 18 survivors from an original crew of about 270.

quiz_question: Which island is the largest in the Mediterranean Sea?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Sicily is the largest island in the Mediterranean Sea. Its layered history of Greek, Roman, Arab, Norman, and Spanish rule has given it one of the richest cultural mixes in the Mediterranean world.

quiz_question: With which country does Gibraltar share its only land border?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Gibraltar is a British Overseas Territory at the southern tip of the Iberian Peninsula. Its only land border is with Spain, while across the Strait of Gibraltar lies Morocco in Africa — making it a place where two continents are visible at once.

quiz_question: Which three nations share the island of Great Britain?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Great Britain is the largest island in Europe. It comprises England, Scotland, and Wales. Northern Ireland is on a separate island (the island of Ireland) and, together with Great Britain, forms the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland.

quiz_question: Which three countries form the core of Scandinavia?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Scandinavia is the cultural and historical region of Northern Europe encompassing Norway, Sweden, and Denmark. Finland and Iceland are often grouped with these in a broader 'Nordic' context, but the core Scandinavian trio is always Norway, Sweden, and Denmark.

quiz_question: How many European countries does the Rhine River flow through?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Rhine flows through Switzerland, Liechtenstein, Austria, Germany, France, and the Netherlands. It is one of Europe's most important commercial waterways and has historically formed political boundaries between nations.

quiz_question: In what century was Croatia first recognized internationally as independent?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Croatia was first internationally recognized as independent on 7 June 879 during the reign of Duke Branimir — over 1,100 years before its modern independence from Yugoslavia in 1991. Tomislav became its first king by 925.

quiz_question: How many tourist arrivals did Croatia attract as of 2019?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Croatia's stunning Adriatic coastline attracted nearly 20 million tourists in 2019, making it one of the most visited countries in Europe relative to its population of under 4 million.

quiz_question: What was the peaceful 1992 split of Czechoslovakia into two nations nicknamed?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, on 31 December 1992 Czechoslovakia peacefully dissolved into the Czech Republic and Slovakia — dubbed the 'Velvet Divorce' to echo the 'Velvet Revolution' of 1989 that ended communist rule without bloodshed.

quiz_question: Which was the only Central/Eastern European country to remain a democracy throughout the interwar period?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, while neighboring states succumbed to authoritarianism in the 1920s–30s, Czechoslovakia maintained parliamentary democracy until the Munich Agreement of 1938 forced Nazi Germany's takeover of Czech lands.

quiz_question: Which was the first Soviet republic to declare independence from the USSR?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Lithuania proclaimed the restoration of its independence on 11 March 1990 — a full year before the Soviet Union's formal dissolution in December 1991. This bold defiance inspired other Soviet republics to follow.

quiz_question: Which medieval state was the largest country in Europe by the 14th century?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Grand Duchy of Lithuania expanded so dramatically in the 13th–14th centuries that it became the largest state in Europe by territory, spanning from the Baltic to near the Black Sea coast.

quiz_question: What event made Serbia the first constitutional monarchy in the Balkans?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Serbian Revolution in the early 19th century established the nation-state as the Balkans' first constitutional monarchy. Serbia gained formal independence from the Ottoman Empire in 1877, recognized by the Treaty of Berlin.

quiz_question: How many UNESCO World Heritage Sites does Romania have?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Romania hosts 11 UNESCO World Heritage Sites and has developed a reputation as a technology center with some of the fastest internet connections globally — an unlikely pairing for a country famous for castles and Dracula tourism.

quiz_question: Which major river empties into the Black Sea at Romania's eastern border?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Romania sits at the lower Danube where it meets the Black Sea, forming the Danube Delta — one of Europe's largest and most biodiverse wetlands and a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
